perturbation.original_text: 5. IDENTIFICATION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information shall be identified prior to disclosure with an appropriate marking or identification such as CONFIDENTIAL or any similar legend. If such information is disclosed either orally or visually, then the Parties will use reasonable efforts to assure protection pursuant to this Agreement. The Parties shall use reasonable effort to reduce such oral or visual Confidential Information to tangible form otherwise in compliance with this Agreement and furnish a copy to the Parties within thirty (30) business days of the original oral or visual disclosure.Company:Electronic signature are available on this document. To use, click o the signature field and follow the prompts.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. IDENTIFICATION OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information should be identified prior to disclosure with an appropriate marking or identification such as CONFIDENTIAL or any similar legend. If such information is disclosed either orally or visually, then the Parties may use reasonable efforts to assure protection pursuant to this Agreement. The Parties may use reasonable effort to reduce such oral or visual Confidential Information to tangible form otherwise in compliance with this Agreement and furnish a copy to the Parties within sixty (60) business days of the original oral or visual disclosure.Company:Electronic signature are available on this document. To use, click o the signature field and follow the prompts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should' and 'will' to 'may' weakens the obligation to protect confidentiality, and increasing the timeframe to provide copies of information introduces conflicting interpretations because the document has the word SHALL written in other sections related to confidentility. This creates uncertainty regarding the protection and handling of confidential information within the document itself, thus an In-Text Contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses "shall" and "will," creating a mandatory obligation for protecting confidential information and providing copies. The changed text weakens this obligation by using "should" and "may," making the protection discretionary. This contradicts Section 6, which states "The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information..." creating a clear in-text contradiction because the obligatory language of Section 6 is undermined by the discretionary language introduced in the changed text, thus adhering to the Ambiguities definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and shall further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. NON-DISCLOSURE: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to any third party individual, corporation, or other entity with prior written consent of the Disclosing Party and may further limit the circulation and disclosure of Confidential Information within its own organization to its employees having a "need to know" the Confidential Information for the purpose set forth in this Agreement, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the confidential nature thereof and agree to and are required to observe the provision of confidentiality set forth herein. The Receiving Party will not attempt to determine the content or structure, or otherwise reverse engineer or decompile any material sample, hardware or software to which it is provided access pursuant to this Agreement except as expressly provided under Section 3 of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not disclose' to 'may disclose' creates direct conflict with the core principle of a non-disclosure agreement. Other sections and the general intent of the document emphasize confidentiality, making this permission to disclose a contradiction and a breach of the document's own purpose. There are other sections of the agreement, such as section 9, that requires a standard of care to not disclose, thus creating an In-Text Contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. STANDARD OF CARE: The Receiving Party shall protect the disclosed Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care that the Receiving Party uses to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 6 stated the Receiving Party 'shall not disclose' Confidential Information, while the changed text states the Receiving Party 'may disclose' Confidential Information. This directly contradicts Section 9, which requires the Receiving Party to protect Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care. 'May disclose' inherently undermines the 'standard of care' to protect the information, as it permits disclosure, creating a direct conflict and in-text contradiction. The Perturbation explanation also points to this exact issue and calls it an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. No copies shall be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Nothwithstanding the foregoing, a Receiving Party may retain a single archival copy of the received Confidential Information which may be used solely for legal evidentiary purposes in the event of a dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: All Confidential Information delivered by either Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Copies may be made without prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. All Confidential Information including the copies made thereof is the sole property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed upon written request by the Disclosing Party. Nothwithstanding the foregoing, a Receiving Party may retain a single archival copy of the received Confidential Information which may be used solely for legal evidentiary purposes in the event of a dispute arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'No copies shall be made without prior written consent' to 'Copies may be made without prior written consent' directly contradicts the established protection of confidential information elsewhere in the agreement. This creates confusion over usage rights, especially considering the 'sole property' statement and other restrictions on disclosure. Sections related to restrictions of usage of the content, have the In-Text Contradiction with this perturbation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. NO LICENSE OR OTHER RIGHTS: No patent, copyright, trademark, or other license express or implied, in the information is granted to Receiving Party other than to use the information in the manner and extent authorized by this Agreement. In addition, under this Agreement, neither Party has an obligation to: a. purchase any services or item from the other party. b. deal exclusively with the other Party in any field. c. offer for sale products using or incorporating the Confidential Information. The Parties do not intend that an agency, partnership, team or joint venture relationship be created between them by this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 8 to allow copies of confidential information to be made without prior written consent, directly contradicting the original requirement for consent. This conflicts with section 10, which grants the Receiving Party the right to use the information only 'in the manner and extent authorized by this Agreement', implying that unauthorized copying is not permitted. The contradiction creates uncertainty regarding the usage rights of the confidential information, impacting the enforceability of the 'No License or Other Rights' clause.

perturbation.original_text: 12. ASSIGNMENT: This Agreement shall be binding upon the Parties, their successors, and assignors. Neither Party shall assign this Agreement nor any Confidential Information received from the other Party pursuant to this Agreement without the other Party's prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. ASSIGNMENT: This Agreement shall be binding upon the Parties, their successors, and assignors. Either Party may assign this Agreement and any Confidential Information received from the other Party pursuant to this Agreement without the other Party's prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Neither Party shall assign' to 'Either Party may assign', the document now has conflicting instructions about assigning the agreement or information. This contradicts the non-transferable nature implied throughout the NDA and causes uncertainty. This becomes and In-Text Contradiction because, within the text, it now has permission to assign from one sentence to another.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. ASSIGNMENT: This Agreement shall be binding upon the Parties, their successors, and assignors. Either Party may assign this Agreement and any Confidential Information received from the other Party pursuant to this Agreement without the other Party's prior written consent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 12, altering 'Neither Party shall assign' to 'Either Party may assign'. This directly contradicts the original intention of restricting assignment and creates conflicting instructions within the same section. The contradicted text, being identical to the changed text, highlights the in-text contradiction. The perturbation type is Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction, which applies because the term 'assign' now has conflicting meanings within the document, making enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 14. LIMITED WARRANTY AND LIABILITY: Discloser warrants that it has the right to disclose Confidential Information to Recipient. Discloser makes no other warranties with respect to the Confidential Information and provides all information "AS IS" without any express or implied warranty of any kind, including any warranty as to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, accuracy, completeness or violation of third party intellectual property rights. Neither Party will be liable for any special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind whatsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or receipt of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. LIMITED WARRANTY AND LIABILITY: Discloser warrants that it may have the right to disclose Confidential Information to Recipient. Discloser makes no other warranties with respect to the Confidential Information and provides all information "AS IS" without any express or implied warranty of any kind, including any warranty as to merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, accuracy, completeness or violation of third party intellectual property rights. Neither Party will be liable for any special, incidental or consequential damages of any kind whatsoever resulting from the disclosure, use or receipt of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Discloser warrants that it has the right' to 'Discloser warrants that it may have the right' introduces ambiguity and undermines the certainty of the discloser's right to share the information. Other sections rely on the assumption of legitimate disclosure, so this creates a fundamental inconsistency. If the discloser isn't able to give such warranty, then there could be breaches in other clauses related to confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. EXCEPTIONS TO CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION: No restriction shall exist under this Agreement with respect to any portion of the Confidential Information that is: a. established by the Receiving Party to have been known by it at the time of receipt and reduced to written form. b. published or otherwise becomes generally known through no wrongful act of the Receiving Party. c. received from a third party without similar restrictions and without breach of the restrictions within this Agreement. d. independently developed by the Receiving Party prior to receipt of the Confidential Information. e. furnished to a third party by the Disclosing Party without similar restriction on the recipients' rights. f. approved in writing for release by the Disclosing Party. g. required by court order or government agency to be disclosed. h. disclosed by inspection of a product incorporating the Confidential Information after the product has been disclosed or sold.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 14, weakening the warranty regarding the discloser's right to disclose confidential information from 'has the right' to 'may have the right'. This introduces ambiguity where certainty was previously established. This change contradicts Section 7, which outlines exceptions to confidential information based on the assumption that the disclosing party has the right to disclose the information in the first place. If the discloser only 'may have the right,' the exceptions listed in Section 7 become questionable because the basis for legitimate disclosure is undermined, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
